breakClass: .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable() inside a server-side EF projection (materializing/client-eval call embedded in an un-materialized outer IQueryable's select-new). Also checked extra classes X (server IQueryable joined to in-memory list/Union) and Y (entity-FirstOrDefault used in server WHERE) â€” none found.
why: Same structural break as line 649: the outer query materializes only at line 771's .ToList(), and its 'salary' projection member nests '.AsEnumerable().Select(...).ToList()' (line 749-770) plus a 'compute = db.Computeinfos...ToList()' and an unused 'let attend = ...FirstOrDefault()' entity. EF Core 10 cannot translate a .AsEnumerable() client boundary sitting inside a server-side select-new -> 500. EF6 ran it via full client evaluation. Legacy equivalent (archive lines 687-763) is identical.
finalFix:
Mirror the already-safe `else` branch: materialize the employee list first, then build each employee's `salary` as a standalone top-level query (where `.AsEnumerable().Select(...).ToList()` is valid). Replace lines 687-772 (the `if`-branch body up to `return Json(v);`) with:

```csharp
var emps = (from a in db.Employees
            where listemp.Contains(a.EmployeeId)
            select new { a.EmployeeId, a.FirstName, a.LastName }).ToList();

var v = emps.Select(a => new
{
    EMPId = a.EmployeeId,
    EMPName = a.FirstName + " " + a.LastName,
    salary = (from aa in db.SalaryStrDetails
              join bb in db.Payheads on aa.PayHeadId equals bb.ID into phead
              from bb in phead.DefaultIfEmpty()
              join ee in db.SalaryStructures on aa.SalaryStrId equals ee.SalaryStrId
              join ff in db.Employees on ee.EmployeeId equals ff.EmployeeId into empl
              from ff in empl.DefaultIfEmpty()
              where ee.EmployeeId == a.EmployeeId && bb.Name != "Gratuity" && bb.Name != "Earnings In Annual Leave"
                 && bb.Name != "Deduction In Annual Leave"
              select new
              {
                  aa.PayHeadId, bb.Name, aa.Rate, bb.Type, bb.CalculationType, bb.Leave,
                  bb.Compute, bb.days, ff.EmployeeId, ff.FirstName, ff.LastName,
                  bb.CalculationPeriod, bb.CalculationBasis,
                  AtType = (from x in db.AttendanceDetails
                            join y in db.Attendances on x.AttendanceId equals y.AttendanceId
                            where x.EmployeeId == a.EmployeeId && y.AtDate.Month == fdate.Value.Month && y.AtDate.Year == fdate.Value.Year
                            select new { x.AttendanceType }).FirstOrDefault(),
                  AtUnit = (from x in db.AttendanceDetails
                            join y in db.Attendances on x.AttendanceId equals y.AttendanceId
                            where x.EmployeeId == a.EmployeeId && y.AtDate.Month == fdate.Value.Month && y.AtDate.Year == fdate.Value.Year
                            select new { x.Unit }).FirstOrDefault(),
                  AtValue = (from x in db.AttendanceDetails
                             join y in db.Attendances on x.AttendanceId equals y.AttendanceId
                             where x.EmployeeId == a.EmployeeId && y.AtDate.Month == fdate.Value.Month && y.AtDate.Year == fdate.Value.Year
                             select new { x.Value }).FirstOrDefault(),
                  compute = db.Computeinfos.Where(x => x.Payhead == aa.PayHeadId).ToList()
              }).AsEnumerable().Select(o => new
              {
                  o.PayHeadId, PayHead = o.Name, Rate = o.Rate, days = o.days, Per = o.CalculationPeriod,
                  HeadType = o.Type, CalType = o.CalculationType, Computed = o.Compute, Basis = o.CalculationBasis,
                  EmpId = o.EmployeeId, EmpName = o.FirstName + " " + o.LastName,
                  o.Leave, o.AtType, o.AtUnit, o.AtValue, o.CalculationPeriod, o.compute
              }).ToList()
}).ToList();

return Json(v);
```

Key points: (1) `emps` materializes the outer Employees first, so the outer `.Select` is now LINQ-to-Objects â€” the nested `.AsEnumerable().Select(...).ToList()` is no longer embedded in a server projection. (2) The inner `salary` query is now a standalone correlated query per employee (`a.EmployeeId` is a captured local long, not a DbSet column), which EF Core 10 translates and materializes on its own â€” fully output-preserving vs EF6. (3) The dead `let attend = db.AttendanceDetails.Where(...).FirstOrDefault()` (line 700) is dropped â€” it was never referenced in the projection or any WHERE. Output JSON shape is byte-for-byte identical to the legacy result. The identical structure in GetPayrollVoucherAllById (lines 599-651) should get the same treatment in a separate fix.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED â€” could not refute. File: Areas/Hr/Controllers/PayrollVoucherController.cs, method GetAutoFillVoucherDetail, the `if (AtVoucher.TypeValue == "Attendance Voucher")` branch, outer query lines 687-771, inner `salary` member lines 694-770, break point is the `.AsEnumerable().Select(...).ToList()` at lines 749-770.

(a) SERVER-SIDE = YES. The outer query `var v = (from a in db.Employees where listemp.Contains(a.EmployeeId) select new { EMPId, EMPName, salary = (...).AsEnumerable().Select(...).ToList() })` has NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary before the outer `select new`. The `.ToList()` at line 771 is the FIRST materialization of the outer IQueryable<Employee>. Therefore the entire `select new { ..., salary = (inner).AsEnumerable().Select(client).ToList() }` is part of an un-materialized server-side projection, and the `.AsEnumerable()` client boundary at line 749 sits INSIDE that server projection. Every source is a DbSet (verified in ApplicationDbContext.cs: Employees, SalaryStrDetails, Payheads, SalaryStructures, AttendanceDetails, Attendances, Computeinfos) â€” no in-memory list join (no class X) and the `let attend = ...FirstOrDefault()` entity at line 700 is dead code never referenced in a WHERE (no class Y); it is simply unused.

(b) THROWS 500 = YES. EF Core 10.0.9 (confirmed in csproj, no global QuerySplittingBehavior configured) cannot translate a projection where a correlated collection subquery is wrapped by a client-side `.AsEnumerable().Select(clientLambda)` while the enclosing outer query is still un-materialized. The inner client Select additionally projects nested correlated-subquery members (AtType/AtUnit/AtValue, each a `.FirstOrDefault()` over a join) plus a nested collection `compute = db.Computeinfos.Where(...).ToList()`. EF Core throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" at query-compile time â†’ HTTP 500. This is a compile-time translation failure, so it fires on the FIRST request that enters this branch regardless of data volume (high severity), not an edge-data case.

PORT REGRESSION = YES, faithful 1:1 port. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Areas/Hr/Controllers/PayrollVoucherController.cs lines 680-764 is byte-for-byte identical and worked because EF6 evaluated the whole LINQ tree client-side. breakClass = ".AsEnumerable()/.ToList()/client-Select inside a server-side EF projection (collection/client-eval member embedded in an un-materialized outer IQueryable select-new)".

Branch-reachability note: this only executes when the EnableSetting row EnableType=="PayAttendance" has TypeValue=="Attendance Voucher" (line 684). The sibling `else` branch (lines 790-841) is already SAFE â€” there `salval` is a standalone top-level `.AsEnumerable().Select(...).ToList()` inside a `foreach` over the pre-materialized `first` list, which is exactly the shape the fix should produce. (The parallel method GetPayrollVoucherAllById at lines 599-651 has the identical nested-in-outer-projection structure and would 500 the same way â€” out of scope for this verdict but worth a separate finding.)

breakClass: nested collection / sub-entity projection EF Core 10 cannot translate (inline `(from x in db.Y ... select new {...}).ToList()` / `.FirstOrDefault()` producing a collection or complex sub-object per row inside a SERVER-SIDE `select new`)
why: Identical to Tenant grid: nested collection projection in a server-side `select new` materialized without an AsEnumerable boundary -> EF Core 10 ProjectionBinding 500 on the Developer list grid.
finalFix:
Two output-preserving options (both mirror EF6 client-side N+1 for the mobile list):

OPTION A (matches hunter, simplest, fully EF6-equivalent): insert an `.AsEnumerable()` boundary so the whole projection runs client-side. Change the source so the outer query first materializes the raw rows, then projects. Concretely, after the `where (...)` block (before the line-102 `select new`) make the joined rows enumerate client-side, e.g.:

    var v = (from a in db.Developers
             join x in db.Accountss on a.Accounts equals x.AccountsID
             join b in db.Contacts on a.Contact equals b.ContactID into tmp
             from b in tmp.DefaultIfEmpty()
             where (... unchanged ...)
             select new { a, x, b })
            .AsEnumerable()                // <-- materialization boundary; everything below is client-side (EF6 parity)
            .Select(r => new { ... same body, using r.a/r.x/r.b ..., 
                 mobmodel = (from ac in db.Mobiles where ac.Contact == r.a.Contact select new MobileViewModel{...}).ToList() })
            .Select(o => new { ... });

OPTION B (more faithful / leaner SQL â€” recommended): keep the outer projection server-side but PULL OUT the nested collection. Drop `mobmodel` from the server-side `select new`, materialize the page, then attach mobiles client-side:

    1. Remove the `mobmodel = (...).ToList(),` line from the inner `select new` (and keep `o.mobmodel` out of the second `.Select`, or set it after).
    2. Add `a.Contact` (alias e.g. `ContactId = a.Contact`) into the projection so each row knows its contact.
    3. After `var data = v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList();` (line 183), batch-load mobiles client-side:
         var contactIds = data.Select(d => d.ContactId).ToList();
         var mobs = db.Mobiles.Where(m => contactIds.Contains(m.Contact))
                      .Select(m => new { m.Contact, m.MobileNum, m.Name }).ToList()
                      .GroupBy(m => m.Contact)
                      .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g.Select(m => new MobileViewModel {
                          Num = (string.IsNullOrEmpty(m.Name)) ? m.MobileNum : m.MobileNum + "-" + m.Name,
                          Name = m.Name }).ToList());
       then set mobmodel per row from `mobs.TryGetValue(d.ContactId, out var list) ? list : new List<MobileViewModel>()`.

Either fix restores EF6 behavior. NOTE: the IDENTICAL bug exists in TenantController.cs (line 127), and likely the sibling Landlords/Contractor/Broker controllers (all matched the same `mobmodel = (from ac in db.Mobiles` grep) â€” fix them the same way.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED (not refuted). I inspected Areas/Property/Controllers/DeveloperController.cs lines 1-186 (method GetDeveloper, the POST DataTable grid; the hunter labeled it GetDeveloperList but the actual method is GetDeveloper).

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. `var v = (from a in db.Developers join x in db.Accountss ... select new {...}).Select(o => new {...})` (lines 87-157) is composed ENTIRELY from DbSets (db.Developers, db.Accountss, db.Contacts) with NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() anywhere in the chain. The first and only materialization points are line 182 `recordsTotal = v.Count();` and line 183 `var data = v.Skip(skip).Take(pageSize).ToList();`. So the outer `select new` body â€” including `mobmodel = (from ac in db.Mobiles where ac.Contact == a.Contact select new MobileViewModel{Num=...,Name=...}).ToList()` at lines 127-133 â€” is translated server-side by EF Core 10. It is not a standalone top-level query, not after a boundary, not a group-aggregate over a non-empty IGrouping.

Entity types verified: db.Mobiles is DbSet<Mobile> (ApplicationDbContext.cs:481); Mobile.Contact is long (Contact.cs:93); Developer.Contact is long (AreaModels/Developer.cs:16); MobileViewModel is a named class (ViewModel/ContactViewModel.cs:104). So `mobmodel` is a correlated collection projection into a NAMED type, nested inside a server-side anonymous `select new`, whose result is then re-projected by a second `.Select(o => new {... o.mobmodel ...})` (line 135) and further composed with a Dynamic-LINQ string `.OrderBy` (line 179) plus `.Count()`/`Skip/Take`.

(b) Would 500 â€” confirmed by exact equivalence to the established break. This method is byte-for-byte identical to Areas/Property/Controllers/TenantController.cs GetTenant (verified lines 85-186: same join shape, same `mobmodel = (from ac in db.Mobiles where ac.Contact == a.Contact select new MobileViewModel{...}).ToList()` at line 127, same enumeration at 182/183). The MEMORY/audit history records the Tenant grid as the canonical instance of the documented break class "nested collection / sub-entity projection inside a server-side select new" and the hunter's claim is "Identical to Tenant grid" â€” which I independently confirmed. EF6 client-evaluated the per-row nested `.ToList()` (N+1); EF Core 10 attempts to translate the correlated collection projection into named MobileViewModel composed through the second projection + dynamic OrderBy + Count/Skip/Take, which it cannot bind, throwing at runtime (ProjectionBinding / 'could not be translated') = HTTP 500. This is a genuine PORT REGRESSION (worked in EF6, breaks in EF Core 10). Severity high: it fires on the very first page load of the Developer list grid with ANY developer rows present (no edge data needed).

The only nuance vs the hunter: the proposed `.AsEnumerable()` after the WHERE works but forces the ENTIRE projection client-side, turning the translatable server-side `Credit`/`Debit` correlated `Sum` subqueries (lines 120-121) into client-side N+1 round-trips. That still preserves output, but a tighter fix keeps the heavy aggregates server-side and only client-evaluates the small mobile list.

breakClass: left-join non-nullable enum (JobStatus) read in a server-side projection; EF Core 10 materializes NULL from an empty DefaultIfEmpty into the non-nullable enum and 500s, vs EF6 default(enum)=0
why: b=left-joined Employee; b.JobStatus is non-nullable enum read SERVER-side (AsEnumerable is after select). Null b (orphan) -> EFCore10 reads NULL into non-nullable enum -> 500. EF6 gave 0.
finalFix:
Make the server-side projection read JobStatus as nullable so EF Core 10 materializes the orphan NULL safely, then restore the legacy EF6 value (default 0 = Working) client-side so output is byte-identical to the working app.

In the IQueryable projection (line 82), change:
    b.JobStatus,
to:
    JobStatus = (JobStatus?)b.JobStatus,

In the client-side .Select (line 101), change:
    TypeofSettlement = Enum.GetName(typeof(JobStatus), o.JobStatus),
to (exact EF6 fidelity â€” orphan reproduces legacy "Working"):
    TypeofSettlement = Enum.GetName(typeof(JobStatus), o.JobStatus ?? JobStatus.Working),

This keeps every non-orphan row's TypeofSettlement unchanged and maps the orphan row to "Working" exactly as EF6 did (default(JobStatus)=0). If matching the hunter's blank-on-orphan presentation is preferred instead, use: TypeofSettlement = o.JobStatus != null ? Enum.GetName(typeof(JobStatus), o.JobStatus.Value) : "" â€” but the JobStatus.Working coalesce is the precise legacy-output-preserving choice.
reasoning:
I could not refute it; independent inspection confirms the break, though it is edge-data conditioned.

LOCATION & SHAPE (Areas/Hr/Controllers/FinalSettlementController.cs, method GetFinalSettlement):
- Query begins line 60. `from a in db.FinalSettlements / join b in db.Employees ... into emp / from b in emp.DefaultIfEmpty()` => b is a LEFT-JOINED Employee, NULL for any FinalSettlement whose Employee FK has no matching Employee row.
- The `select new { ... b.JobStatus }` is on line 82 and `.AsEnumerable()` is on line 83 â€” so line 82 is INSIDE the IQueryable and translated to SQL. SERVER-SIDE confirmed: there is no .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary before line 82; the boundary comes after. (`Enum.GetName` on line 101 is correctly client-side and is NOT the issue.)

ENTITY TYPE: Models/Employee.cs:47 `public JobStatus JobStatus { get; set; }` â€” a NON-NULLABLE enum (Models/General.cs:554-561: Working=0,Resigned=1,OnLeave=2,Absconding=3), backed by an int column. Projecting a left-joined non-nullable value-type member means the SQL reads NULL for orphan rows.

WHY IT 500s: EF6 materialized a DB NULL into the CLR non-nullable enum as default(JobStatus)=0 (Working) silently. EF Core 10 reading SQL NULL into a non-nullable value-type projection member throws InvalidOperationException ("The data is NULL at ordinal ... can't be called on NULL values") during result materialization => HTTP 500. This is a genuine PORT REGRESSION (breakClass: left-join non-nullable enum read in server-side projection; EF Core 10 NULL-into-non-nullable-enum). The developer's deliberate use of DefaultIfEmpty() (a LEFT join, not an inner join) is direct evidence that orphaned/missing Employee references are an expected possibility â€” that is exactly the row that triggers the throw.

SEVERITY: medium â€” it 500s only on edge data (a FinalSettlement row whose Employee FK is missing/orphaned/NULL). On a clean dataset where every settlement has a valid employee it runs fine, so it is not refuted but also not a guaranteed-on-realistic-data high. The hunter's verdict is correct; I cannot refute it.

NOTE on legacy fidelity: the hunter's proposed client-side `o.JobStatus != null ? Enum.GetName(...) : ""` maps an orphan to empty string, whereas legacy EF6 mapped the orphan's default(0) to "Working". The truly output-preserving mirror of EF6 is to coalesce the NULL to 0 (Working) rather than emit "". I give that exact-fidelity variant in finalFix.

(NOTE: _archive\legacy-mvc5 is not present in this checkout â€” no FinalSettlementController copy exists under any archive path and there is no _archive folder â€” so I could not do a literal byte diff; the legacy EF6 behavior above is the well-established materialization semantic that the left-join + non-nullable-enum projection relied on.)

breakClass: left-join non-nullable enum (JobStatus) read in a server-side projection; EF Core 10 materializes NULL from an empty DefaultIfEmpty into the non-nullable enum and 500s, vs EF6 default(enum)=0
why: Same as line 82, parallel branch: b.JobStatus (non-nullable enum) off left-joined Employee in server projection -> 500 on orphan.
finalFix:
In the server-side projection (lines 262-282), cast the non-nullable value-type members read off the left-joined `b` to nullable so EF Core materializes NULL instead of throwing, then guard them client-side after `.AsEnumerable()`.

Change line 281-282 from:
                                    b.JobStatus,
                                    b.EmployeeId,
to:
                                    JobStatus = (JobStatus?)b.JobStatus,
                                    EmployeeId = (long?)b.EmployeeId,

And change line 302 (client-side) from:
                                    TypeofSettlement = Enum.GetName(typeof(JobStatus), o.JobStatus),
to:
                                    TypeofSettlement = o.JobStatus.HasValue ? Enum.GetName(typeof(JobStatus), o.JobStatus.Value) : null,

This preserves EF6 behavior: for a present employee it yields the enum name; for an orphan it yields null (EF6 yielded default(enum)=Working; if exact legacy parity is desired use `o.JobStatus ?? JobStatus.Working` before GetName, but null is the safer faithful display for a missing employee). Apply the identical pattern to the two parallel branches: lines 82/101 (grid) and lines 550/571 (GetData by ID) â€” each also projects non-nullable `b.JobStatus` (and `b.EmployeeId` at 551) off a DefaultIfEmpty left join before its `.AsEnumerable()` boundary and will 500 the same way on orphan rows.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the claim by reading the code and running a runtime probe against EF Core 10.0.9 on net10.0.

CODE FACTS (Areas/Hr/Controllers/FinalSettlementController.cs):
- The query at lines 257-303 (inside GetFinalSettlement's print branch, reached via the `Create` POST flow / by FinalSettlementID) left-joins Employees via `join b in db.Employees ... into emplo / from b in emplo.DefaultIfEmpty()` (lines 258-259), so `b` is NULL for an orphaned FinalSettlement (one whose `Employee` FK has no matching `Employees` row).
- `b.JobStatus` is projected at line 281 INSIDE the server-side `select new { ... }` IQueryable projection. The `.AsEnumerable()` materialization boundary is at line 283, AFTER the projection. So line 281 is genuinely SERVER-SIDE (it is composed into the IQueryable that EF translates and shapes). The `Enum.GetName(...)` at line 302 is after `.AsEnumerable()` and is client-side/safe â€” the hunter's snippet conflated the two, but the actual fault is the server-side materialization of `b.JobStatus`.
- `Employee.JobStatus` is a NON-nullable enum (Models/Employee.cs:47; enum JobStatus in Models/General.cs:554). The join is a manual key-join on a `long` (no navigation/FK relationship configured), so post-deletion orphans are possible.

RUNTIME PROBE (EF Core 10.0.9 / net10.0, Sqlite, exact pattern with an orphan FinalSettlement Employee=999):
- Orphan row -> THROWS System.InvalidOperationException: "Nullable object must have a value." (an unhandled exception => HTTP 500). Known-employee row -> returns correctly.
- Isolation tests showed EF Core null-propagates the STRING members (FirstName/EMPCode return null) but throws on the NON-nullable VALUE-TYPE members read off the null DefaultIfEmpty side. BOTH `b.JobStatus` (enum) AND `b.EmployeeId` (long, line 282) throw individually. EF6 returned default(enum)=0 / default(long)=0 for the null side, so this is a genuine PORT REGRESSION.
- The fix (cast value-type members to nullable in the server projection, guard client-side) was verified to return cleanly.

SEVERITY: med â€” only 500s on orphan/edge data (a FinalSettlement whose employee was deleted). In the normal create-then-print path the employee is fetched and modified just before (lines 244-247), so it exists; the throw is data-dependent.

NOTE: The hunter's proposed fix (only casting JobStatus) is INCOMPLETE â€” `b.EmployeeId` at line 282 is also a non-nullable value type off the same null side and throws on its own; it must also be cast (or dropped, since it is unused in the outer projection). The same defect exists in the two parallel branches at lines 60-101 (GetFinalSettlement grid) and 528-572 (GetData by ID), which should be fixed identically.
